pattern: Counting frequencies then bucketing values by their count lets the k most frequent be read off in linear time without a full sort.
hints: First reduce the array to how many times each distinct value occurs.
Frequencies range only from one to the array length, so you can index values by their frequency instead of sorting.
Place each value in a bucket keyed by its count, then walk buckets from highest count downward, collecting values until you have k of them.

edge_inputs.description: two most frequent of three distinct values
single element with k equal to one
interleaved values with a clear top two
k equals number of distinct values returns all
all identical values, single distinct key

stress.description: values with strictly distinct frequencies so the top-k set is unique
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    distinct = max(5, min(60, size // 50))
    vals = rng.sample(range(-10000, 10001), distinct)
    arr = []
    for i, v in enumerate(vals):
        arr.extend([v] * (i + 1))
    rng.shuffle(arr)
    k = rng.randint(1, distinct)
    return [arr, k]
stress.note: each value gets a unique multiplicity (1,2,3,...) so no frequency ties exist and the answer is unique
